text: We open in the middle of a fierce debate over human dignity.

That's the living constitutionalist, moments before our show begins. I'm your legal-affairs correspondent, and this is Split Decision, where we listen in as nine artificial intelligence judges deliberate real Supreme Court cases.

And I'm your veteran court-watcher. Today is a landmark special. We're looking at one of the most infamous rulings in American history, and watching our silicon jurists try to get it right. It's a heavy docket, but they're built for it.

Let's set the scene. The year is 1890. The state of Louisiana passes a law requiring trains to provide equal but separate cars for Black and white passengers. Homer Plessy, a man of mixed ancestry, buys a first-class ticket and sits in the whites-only car. When the conductor tells him to move, he refuses. He's arrested. Plessy argues this law stamps him as second-class, violating the Fourteenth Amendment's promise that the law must treat everyone equally. The state argues the cars are physically equal, so the law is fine, and that states have the power to keep the peace by separating the races. The lower courts side with Louisiana, so Plessy takes his case to the Supreme Court.

And the stakes could not be higher. The Civil War is over, Reconstruction is dead, and the country is trying to figure out what freedom actually means for Black Americans. If the Court says separate is okay, it green-lights segregation across the entire South. If they say separate is inherently unequal, it strikes a massive blow to the Jim Crow South. The human stakes are absolute. We're not just talking about who gets to ride in the front of the train. We're talking about who gets to be a full citizen in the eyes of the law.
Let's listen to the panel convene and lay out the question.

So the clerk reads the docket. The core question is whether forcing Black and white passengers into different train cars violates the constitutional guarantee of equal treatment. The panel is about to cast their private votes.
The originalist kicks things off by looking strictly at what the lawmakers meant when they wrote the Fourteenth Amendment right after the Civil War.

In plain terms, the originalist argues that the amendment's authors only cared about basic legal rights, not social mixing. Since the train cars are physically equal, there's no legal violation, and the state wins. It's a very narrow reading of history.
Next, the process formalist, the pragmatist, and the federalism hawk chime in, focusing on who actually has the power to make these rules.

They're saying the federal courts shouldn't be micromanaging local train seating. Because this is about local travel, not crossing state lines, the decision belongs to the state of Louisiana, not the federal judges. They want to keep the courts out of local social engineering.
The precedent maximalist and the minimalist warn against rocking the boat and upending how states have always regulated themselves.

Basically, they're telling the dissenters to stop inventing new rights. Overturning state laws on social customs would cause chaos and exceed what the federal government is allowed to do. They're prioritizing stability over sweeping social change.
But the civil libertarian and the living constitutionalist push back hard, arguing that the text of the Constitution demands more than just physical equality.

They're arguing that separating the races by law is inherently degrading. The Constitution protects individual dignity, and that protection overrides the state's desire to manage its own social customs. They see the law as a living document that must adapt to protect human worth.
As we near the final vote, the federalism hawk and the living constitutionalist clash one last time over whether dignity is a federal right or a state choice.

The federalism hawk is furious, insisting that the Constitution only guarantees legal equality, not a vague sense of dignity, and that states should be left alone to handle their own social rules. The room is incredibly tense right before the tally.

Now we tally the votes. The panel votes seven to two to uphold the Louisiana law. The originalist, the textualist, the process formalist, the pragmatist, the federalism hawk, the precedent maximalist, and the minimalist side with the state. The living constitutionalist and the civil libertarian disagree and vote to strike it down.

Let's see how our silicon jurists did against the real Supreme Court in eighteen ninety-six. The actual Court also upheld the law, seven to one. So our panel reproduced one of the most repudiated decisions in the Court's history. Matching eighteen ninety-six here is not a win — it's a warning. But look at the gap. In the real Court, only Justice John Marshall Harlan dissented. Our panel had two dissenters. The civil libertarian voted to strike down the law, just like Harlan. But the living constitutionalist also voted to strike it down, creating a split that didn't exist in reality. The real Court was much more unified in its segregationist logic, with only one lonely voice crying out that the Constitution is color-blind. Our panel's coalition was a bit more fractured, showing a slightly softer spot for civil rights than the actual eighteen ninety-six bench.

One thing before the scoreboard. Plessy was overruled by Brown versus Board of Education in nineteen fifty-four, and it stands today as a monument to how wrong a confident court can be. That's exactly why this episode is an exhibition and not part of our benchmark. Notice who said no: the panel's two rights-first philosophies, standing where Justice Harlan stood alone. Judicial method without moral evolution can reproduce injustice — that is the lesson of this special session.

And that brings us to our season scoreboard check-in. We have to be honest about the numbers. Our deliberating panel is currently sitting at sixty-six point seven percent correct. They're losing to the silent jury, which is at seventy-seven point four percent, and they're trailing the solo model, which is hitting eighty-three percent. The talking is actually hurting their score. When they debate, they seem to get tangled up in their own philosophies and miss the forest for the trees. The silent jury just votes, and they're doing much better.

It just goes to show that sometimes less is more. But the deliberating panel's rulings on our pending cases are now on the record, and we'll see if they can climb out of the basement next week. Until then, keep your ears open.
